title: /explain explain recursive thinking from the profe

parts.text: /explain explain recursive thinking from the professor's perspective

## Recursive Thinking: The Professor's Perspective

### What Dr. Marty Folsom Means by Recursion

From the Class 2 slides on "Theology for Psychology," Dr. Folsom presents recursive thinking as a foundational concept for understanding the relational self and deep interpersonal connection.

### The Core Definition

Recursion: Thinking about you, thinking about me, thinking about you...

This is not just thinking about another person. It's thinking about what the other person is thinking about you while you're thinking about them. It's a mirroring process that goes deeper than simple recognition.

### The Levels of Knowing (Building Toward Recursion)

Dr. Folsom distinguishes recursion from simpler forms of thinking:

- Cognition: Me thinking (I want / don't want)
- Metacognition: Me thinking about thinking (I want to be the kind of person...)
- Recognition: Thinking about a known other, previously known (I have been in this situation before...)
- Recursion: Thinking about you, thinking about me, thinking about you... (I want a deep relationship with this person)

### The Visual Image: Two Mirrors Facing Each Other

From the Class 2 slides, Dr. Folsom uses the metaphor of mirrors:

- One mirror: see self (Individual perspective)
- Two mirrors in the same direction: separated selves in the world
- Two mirrors facing each other: recursion (infinite reflection, depth)

When two mirrors face each other, you get that infinite tunnel effect - each mirror reflects the other reflecting the other. This is what happens in deep relationship.

### Recursion in Practice: The Love Example

Dr. Folsom shares this beautiful illustration from the slides:

"When I fall in love and the moment I can feel that you feel that way too, is when I fall in love with you."

It's not just "I love you." It's "I feel that you feel love for me, and that mutual recognition is what creates the bond."

### The Three Basics of Relationship (Recursive Framework)

From the Class 2 slides, Dr. Folsom outlines what recursion reveals:

- What I feel about myself (Congruence & revelation)
- What I feel about you (Empathy)
- What I think or feel you think of me (Recursion)

This third element is the recursive one - it requires you to enter into the other person's perspective of you.

### When Recursion Is Missing

Dr. Folsom identifies situations where recursion breaks down:

- Mirrors opposite (turned away from each other)
- Mirrors covered (hidden, unable to reflect)
- Missing recursion creates relational dysfunction

### Why This Matters for Counseling

According to the slides, recursive knowing requires you to show up for a trustworthy relationship. As a counselor, you must:

- Be present not just physically but personally
- Create space for the client to feel seen and known
- Recognize that healing happens when the client experiences being thought about, cared for, and held in mind by another

### The Theological Foundation

Dr. Folsom roots this in trinitarian theology. From the Class 2 slides:

"Jesus, I live in You - Recursion in action"

God thinks about us thinking about God. The divine relationship is itself recursive - the Father, Son, and Spirit exist in eternal mutual knowing and indwelling.

### Clinical Application

From Anderson's textbook (Chapter 2), the integrative self requires seeing the whole person in relation. Recursion is the mechanism by which we:

- Move beyond observing symptoms to knowing persons
- Create the therapeutic alliance
- Enable clients to experience being truly known
- Model what healthy relationship looks like

### The Poetic Expression

Dr. Folsom includes e.e. cummings' poem "i carry your heart with me" as an example of recursive love - the beloved is carried within, thought about constantly, part of the self's internal world.

---

**Want me to quiz you on this?** This concept is central to Dr. Folsom's relational theology and shows up in understanding the therapeutic relationship, the nature of personhood, and the image of God.

input.question: Question 1: According to Dr. Folsom's framework, what is the key difference between "recognition" and "recursion" as forms of thinking?
options: A. Recognition is thinking about a known other from the past; recursion is thinking about what the other person thinks about you
B. Recognition requires two people; recursion can happen alone
C. Recognition is cognitive; recursion is emotional
D. Recognition is conscious; recursion is unconscious
input.explanation: From the Class 2 slides, Dr. Folsom distinguishes recognition as "thinking about a known other, previously known (I have been in this situation before...)" while recursion is "thinking about you, thinking about me, thinking about you... (I want a deep relationship with this person)." Recursion involves the mirroring awareness of what the other thinks about you, not just awareness of the other.
input.source: Class 2 Slides: Theology for Psychology Session 2

parts.text: My answer: A. Recognition is thinking about a known other from the past; recursion is thinking about what the other person thinks about you. I got it right. Next question please.

input.question: Question 2: Dr. Folsom uses the metaphor of mirrors to illustrate recursion. Which mirror arrangement represents TRUE recursion?
options: A. One mirror - you see yourself
B. Two mirrors in the same direction - separated selves in the world
C. Two mirrors facing each other - infinite reflection creating depth
D. Two mirrors covered - hidden from view
input.explanation: From the Class 2 slides, Dr. Folsom explicitly states that recursion occurs with "Two mirrors facing each other - recursion." This creates the infinite tunnel effect where each mirror reflects the other reflecting the other, symbolizing the depth of mutual knowing in relationship. One mirror only shows the individual self, and two mirrors in the same direction show separated selves, not recursive connection.
input.source: Class 2 Slides: Theology for Psychology Session 2
